title: Church Government, Part 5: One or Two Kinds of Elders? Teaching and Ruling Elders
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Review: Where We Have Been
subpoints: Five lessons into the Church Government series; this lesson addresses the question: one or two kinds of elders?
Review of previous topics: authority and submission; authority of command vs. counsel; models of church government
Christ is the only head of the church; elder, bishop, and pastor are synonyms
God appointed a plurality of elders (at least two) to rule each local church
Acts 14:23 and James 5:14 are the key passages for the plurality

outline.title: Historical Views on the Question of One or Two Kinds of Elders
subpoints: The Westminster Assembly identified three distinct roles within the eldership: pastor/minister, teacher/doctor, and church governor/elder
The Scottish commissioners (5 of 160) argued that all elders occupy the same office with equal presbyteral status, differing only in duties—they were ultimately persuasive
John Calvin identified five offices: ministers, presbyters/elders, bishops/pastors, teachers, and church governors—essentially a similar schema to the Assembly
Charles Hodge held that 'presbyteros' applies only to ministers; he denied the divine origin of the ruling elder office, seeing it as a pragmatic borrowing from the synagogue
Today, Presbyterian denominations use varying terminology: BPC uses 'ministers, ruling elders, deacons'; PCA uses 'teaching and ruling elder'; OPC uses 'ministers, ruling elders, deacons'

outline.title: The Key Text: 1 Timothy 5:17 and the Word 'Malista'
subpoints: 1 Timothy 5:17: 'Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor, especially (malista) those who labor in the word and doctrine'
The word 'malista' (especially) singles out a subgroup from a whole group; the subgroup shares characteristics with the whole group but has an additional distinguishing characteristic
Illustration: all the military-connected men in the congregation form one group; those currently on active duty form a subgroup
Application: all elders rule well—that is the whole group; 'especially those who labor in word and doctrine' are a subgroup who rule AND preach/teach publicly
This creates a duality in the office: all elders rule; some elders also labor in preaching and teaching

outline.title: The Word 'Malista' in Context—Six Other Uses
subpoints: Galatians 6:10: 'Do good to all, especially (malista) those of the household of faith'—all people are the whole group; the household of faith is the subgroup
Philippians 4:22: 'All the saints greet you, especially (malista) those who are of Caesar's household'—all saints are the whole group; those in Caesar's household are the subgroup
1 Timothy 4:10: 'Christ is Savior of all men, especially (malista) of those who believe'—all men are the group; believers are the subgroup with a special saving relationship
1 Timothy 5:8: 'Anyone who does not provide for his own, especially (malista) those of his own household'—relatives are the whole group; household members are the subgroup
The pattern is consistent: 'malista' introduces a subgroup that shares the characteristics of the larger group but has an additional, distinguishing attribute

outline.title: What the Distinction Means in Practice
subpoints: All elders are called to rule—no elder's vote counts more than another's; when the session meets, there is parity
Some elders are called to rule AND labor in preaching and teaching publicly—these are what the modern church calls 'pastors'
1 Timothy 3:2 says elders must be 'able to teach'—but this does not mean all elders are called to preach publicly
Being 'able to teach' (didaktikos) means every elder must know the Bible well enough to disciple, mentor, counsel, and refute heresy one-on-one

Titus 1:9 clarifies: 'holding fast the faithful word as he has been taught, that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and convict those who contradict'—this is the one-on-one work required of all elders

outline.title: Conclusion and Preview of Next Lesson
subpoints: Scripture teaches a plurality of biblically qualified elders—all who shepherd the flock of God in the local church will rule well
Some will rule well and labor in preaching and teaching publicly—both kinds of elders are God's gifts to His church
Both kinds of elders should be present in every local church
The next lesson will address the qualifications for elders and deacons according to God's Word
It is not enough to have a plurality of elders; these elders must be biblically qualified

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro devotes lesson five of the Church Government series to the question of whether there is one kind of elder or two. After a thorough review of the previous four lessons, he surveys the historical debate within Presbyterian circles: the Westminster Assembly identified three roles (pastor/minister, teacher, and church governor), the Scottish commissioners pressed for full parity within one office, John Calvin similarly identified multiple functions, and Charles Hodge controversially denied the divine origin of the ruling elder office entirely. Today, all mainstream Presbyterian bodies acknowledge some functional distinction within the eldership, even if they label it differently.

The heart of the lesson is a detailed grammatical study of 1 Timothy 5:17 and the key Greek word 'malista' (translated 'especially'). Pastor Lyro demonstrates through six parallel New Testament uses—Galatians 6:10, Philippians 4:22, 1 Timothy 4:10, 1 Timothy 5:8, 2 Timothy 4:13, and Titus 1:10-11—that 'malista' consistently introduces a subgroup that shares the characteristics of the whole group but possesses an additional distinguishing characteristic. Applied to 1 Timothy 5:17, this means: all elders rule well (the whole group); some elders also labor in preaching and teaching (the subgroup). This is not a hierarchy of status but a distinction in function and calling.

The lesson addresses the relationship between the universal qualification 'able to teach' (1 Timothy 3:2) and the specific calling to labor in preaching and teaching. Every elder must be able to disciple, mentor, counsel, and refute heresy—this is the one-on-one teaching work described in Titus 1:9. But not every elder is called to the public ministry of the Word on the Lord's Day. The session closes with a preview of the next lesson: the qualifications for elders and deacons according to God's Word—because having a plurality is not sufficient; these elders must also be biblically qualified.
transcript:
## Introduction

This morning I would like for us to start by turning to 1 Timothy chapter 5 and looking at two verses. 1 Timothy 5, reading verses 17 through 20:

*Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in the word and doctrine. For the Scripture says, 'You shall not muzzle an ox while it treads out the grain,' and, 'The laborer is worthy of his wages.' Do not receive an accusation against an elder except from two or three witnesses. Those who are sinning, rebuke in the presence of all, that the rest also may fear.*

What I wanted us to accomplish today is to answer the question: is there one kind of elder or two kinds of elders?

But before we get there, remember where we have come so far. This is our fifth lesson on the subject of church government—what has Christ appointed for His church?

We saw that we studied the concept of authority and submission. We saw what authority is, what submission is. We also saw how good authority works. We saw that there are authority of council and authority of command. There are some authorities that if they say "jump," you say "how high" on the way up—that's authority of command. And there's authority of counsel, where the difference is what the Bible says concerning that authority's ability to force you to do what they command.

Then we saw the different models of church government: Episcopacy (rule by bishops), Independent Congregationalism (democratic rule by the congregation), the Single Pastor model, New England/Puritan Congregationalism (independent churches ruled by elders), and Presbyterianism (the right way, the one given to us by God). Then we took a brief look at what the BPC Form of Government says. And the most important thing we've seen so far is that Christ is the only head of the church—He gets to tell us what His church is to be and to do.

Then last week we took a look at the terms elder, bishop, and pastor, and we saw they refer to the same office—they're not talking about different people in the church but the same group. And we saw that God appointed a plurality of elders to rule each local congregation. How many is a plurality? At least two. And then we saw that elders are to rule the church—and by implication, if elders are to rule, the church is to follow.

Any questions about the things we have seen so far?

## Review Quiz

All right, so: review quiz. And as soon as I say quiz, all the eyes go down, nobody's looking at me anymore.

Which passage in Acts was the key passage for showing that there should be at least two elders in each local church? 1423—yes, Acts 14:23, where it says that Paul and Barnabas went back to the churches they had established and appointed elders—plural—in every church—singular.

But there's also a passage in James that is very important. Does anybody remember what that passage in James is? Yes—James 5:14: "Call all the elders of the church"—the elders of your local church. More than one elder, there.

## One or Two Kinds of Elders?

Now, related to the question about a plurality of elders governing the local church is the question: are all elders the same, or is there a differentiation within the office of the elder?

I want to start by showing you different views that have been held concerning this matter—just in the Presbyterian world, among people who believe in a plurality of at least two elders.

### The Westminster Assembly

The Westminster Divines—and when you hear the word 'divines,' that's just the old way of saying scholars or theologians; it's not the Presbyterian version of saints we bow down to, but that's how theologians were called in history. The Westminster Divines, those who formulated the Westminster Confession and Catechisms, identified three distinct offices or roles within the eldership: the pastor/minister, the teacher/doctor, and the church governor/elder.

However, Ian Murray (the Scottish historian who started the Banner of Truth Trust) argues that though the distinction of the minister and the ruling elder into separate offices was what won the day at the Assembly, the Scottish members argued that ruling elders occupied the same office as ministers and were equally presbyters, differing only in their duties. The Scottish members—only five out of 160 or so—could not vote, but they were very persuasive.

### John Calvin

Earlier than the Westminster Assembly, John Calvin (in a kind of anachronistic way) agreed with the Assembly in identifying these offices: ministers, presbyters/elders, bishops/pastors, teachers, and church governors—five categories, but essentially the same approach.

### Charles Hodge

When this question comes to the United States, a big influencer was Princeton professor Charles Hodge in the 19th century. Hodge found only one function to which the term 'presbyteros' should be related: he said that word was only used for ministers. He also said the word 'bishop' is only for ministers, and 'shepherd' is always only for ministers. So Hodge did not recognize the divine origin of the office of ruling elder—he said ruling elders came into the church through the synagogue and were a pragmatic thing that worked out well. And he identified only one passage for a governing role other than ministers: Romans 12:8, where we have all these different gifts listed, one of which is 'the gift of leadership or administration.'

It's very difficult to see this view in the Bible, and it's important that this view makes the important office of ruling elder one instituted by pragmatism rather than Scripture. That doesn't seem consistent with the Scriptures. But that was the view of the northern Presbyterians from which the Bible Presbyterian Church originally came.

### Today's Practice

In practice today: Presbyterian denominations often use the terms 'ruling and teaching elders' or 'minister and ruling elders' to demonstrate that there are differences in function within the eldership. The BPC states: *The ordinary and perpetual officers in the church are ministers, ruling elders, and deacons.* The OPC uses similar language: those who share in the rule of the church may be called elders or presbyters, bishops, or church governors; those who also have been called to labor in word and teaching are called ministers. The PCA calls their pastors 'teaching elders' and the other elders 'ruling elders' in their Form of Government.

No matter how you label it—three offices, two offices, whatever—nobody in the Presbyterian world actually practices 100% equality between the minister and the ruling elder. That's just be honest and clear. At the very least there must be a distinction in function, even if you argue for the same office.

## What the Bible Says: 1 Timothy 5:17 and 'Malista'

So let's look at the Bible—the best place for us to look at to figure out what Christ wants us to do.

There is broad agreement among those who believe in plurality-of-elder rule that some distinction should be made within the eldership. And the Scriptures do teach that there is such a distinction. First Timothy 5:17 teaches that. Look again: *Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in the word and doctrine.*

In this verse, Paul singles out those who labor in preaching and teaching *out of* the 'elders who rule well' category, thereby creating a duality in the office. You have all these elders—they all rule well—and from that group, you have these who *also* labor in word and doctrine.

The key is understanding the word 'especially'—the Greek word 'malista.' What that word says is that it singles out a subgroup from a whole group. All the members of the subgroup are part of the whole group, but the subgroup has something additional that not everyone in the whole group has.

Let me illustrate. *[At this point in the original lesson, Pastor Lyro called up several men from the congregation who had all served in the military, then narrowed the group to those currently on active duty, demonstrating with people how 'malista' works: all the military-connected men form one group; the active duty members are the subgroup who share the military connection but have the additional characteristic of currently serving.]*

To use that word: *All these people have a connection with the military; especially Jordan, Ben, and Brandon.* The subgroup still shares characteristics with the whole group, but has some extra characteristic that defines them. Does it make sense?

Now, let's see if that word actually holds what I'm saying. It's used 12 times in the whole New Testament. Let's look at six of them:

**Galatians 6:10**: *As we have opportunity, let us do good to all, especially to those who are of the household of faith.* What's the big group? All people—'everyone.' What is the subgroup that has something extra in common? The household of faith. So here Paul says the household of faith is a subcategory of 'everyone' that has to be paid special attention.

**Philippians 4:22**: *All the saints greet you, especially those who are of Caesar's household.* What's the big group? All the saints. What's the subgroup? Those believers who are also part of Caesar's household. That characteristic is not shared by all the saints.

**1 Timothy 4:10**: *We trust in the living God, who is the Savior of all men, especially of those who believe.* So Christ is the Savior of all men—there is no other Savior. But there is a special relationship of Christ with a subset of all people: those who believe.

**1 Timothy 5:8**: *But if anyone does not provide for his own, and especially for those of his household...* What is the big group? Relatives—'his own.' What's the subgroup? Those of his household. The immediate family that lives with him—all his relatives don't live with him, but he is especially responsible to provide for those who do.

So you can see the word 'malista' being used consistently to separate a subgroup from a whole group. The subgroup still has a connection and characteristics they share with the whole group, but there's some extra characteristic that defines the subgroup.

Now turn back to 1 Timothy 5:17 and let's apply what we've learned:

*Let elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor*—that's the big group. All elders rule well, because that's the calling they have. And then out of this group of elders who rule well, you have *especially those who labor in the word and doctrine.* Those who labor in word and doctrine are also ruling—they share that with the big group. But they also have something else they're supposed to do: labor in word and doctrine.

Are you with me? So that tells us there are two categories of elders: those who rule, and those who rule and also labor in word and doctrine.

The idea is that **all elders rule**. No matter what your title—whether you're titled 'pastor' or 'elder'—you rule. And that's why when a session is meeting, there is parity. Everyone has the same vote, the same power, the same discussion, the same duties. The only difference is when the minister is laboring in word and doctrine. Preaching and teaching elders are commonly referred to as 'pastors' in the modern church.

This also tells us that all elders are *capable of* teaching, but that **not all elders are called to preach and teach publicly in the church**. Especially those who labor in word and doctrine—those are the ones called to teach and preach publicly.

## What About 1 Timothy 3:2: 'Able to Teach'?

Yes, 1 Timothy 3:2 says that the elder must be 'able to teach' ('didaktikos'). This word is only used twice in the whole New Testament, and it's a difficult word to interpret—half the time outside the New Testament it means 'able to teach,' and the other half it means 'being teachable.' So part of the total meaning of that word is being teachable. But how is 'being able to teach' different from 'laboring in preaching and teaching'?

David Dickson, a 19th-century Scottish Presbyterian ruling elder, suggests that the general qualification of being 'able to teach' means that every elder must have a good knowledge of the Bible and be able to communicate that knowledge in a way that is beneficial to those who hear him.

And Paul clarifies what it means to be 'able to teach' in his instructions to Titus in Titus 1:9: *Holding fast the faithful word as he has been taught, that he may be able, by sound doctrine, both to exhort and convict those who contradict.* The idea is one-on-one: every elder must know the doctrine of the church well enough to effectively defend it. Not every elder is called to preach or teach publicly, but every elder should be able to disciple, mentor, counsel people, and refute heresy.

This is different from the public ministry of the Word on the Lord's Day in the church. Tim Witmer wrote a great book called *The Shepherd Leader* which submits that 'able to teach' means every elder should be able to disciple, should be able to mentor people, should be able to counsel people, and should be able to refute heresy—which is different from the public ministry of the word on the Lord's Day.

## Conclusion

So, the Scriptures teach that a plurality of biblically qualified elders is God's way to shepherd His flock. All elders who shepherd the flock of God in each local church will rule well—that is the calling they have. Some will rule well and labor in preaching and teaching. And both kinds of elders are God's gifts to His church and should be present in every local church.

I'll set it up for next week: it is not enough that a church has a plurality of elders. These elders must be *qualified* according to God's word. And that's what we're going to start looking at next Lord's Day.

Let's pray together. Glorious God, we thank You that You are a God who provides for Your church. We pray for our content to provide for us. In Jesus' name. Amen.
